results.title: The paper introduces a new era in AI where agents learn predominantly from experience to achieve superhuman capabilities. Current AI progress relies heavily on human-generated data, as seen in large language models (LLMs) that perform diverse tasks like writing poetry and diagnosing medical issues. However, imitating human data alone cannot achieve superhuman intelligence in domains like mathematics, coding, and science, as high-quality human data is nearing exhaustion. New insights beyond human understanding require agents to generate their own data through interaction with environments.

results.title: Artificial Intelligence as a Service (AIaaS) products such as OpenAI's GPT-3 API places advanced synthetic media generation capabilities in the hands of a global audience at a fraction of the cost, what does the future hold for AI-assisted social engineering attacks? In our talk, we will present the nuts and bolts of an AIaaS phishing pipeline that was successfully deployed in multiple authorized phishing campaigns. Using both paid and free services, we emulated the techniques that even low-skilled, limited resource actors could adopt to execute effective AI-assisted phishing campaigns at scale.  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tWWhRbzhkrg

results.title: Summary: Although AI systems show impressive abilities on structured evaluations (like coding or math problems), they are still far from demonstrating the creativity, novel insight, and flexible problem‐solving required for true scientific R&D. This suggests that replacing human-led research (a key part of reaching “superintelligence”) will take much longer.

results.title: AI is best understood as "Software 2.0," a new computing paradigm that automates tasks not by hand-coding fixed rules (as in traditional Software 1.0), but by training neural networks through optimization against verifiable objectives. In this framework, the key predictor of whether a job or task will be automated is its verifiability—meaning the task must allow an AI to repeatedly practice in a resettable environment with efficient attempts and clear, automated rewards or feedback. As a result, progress in AI is "jagged": verifiable tasks like math, coding, and puzzles advance rapidly (often surpassing top human experts), while non-verifiable ones involving creativity, strategy, real-world context, or common sense lag significantly behind.

results.title: Current LLM limitations may stem from insufficient scaffolding or training to prioritize connection-making for discoveries, as their pre-training does not strongly incentivize this skill. Scaling up model size or designing specific training environments for discovery-making could potentially improve LLMs' ability to make novel connections. The complexity of setting up such training environments suggests a challenging engineering problem, potentially extending AI development timelines. If LLMs achieve general intelligence, their ability to memorize and connect all human knowledge could lead to transformative capabilities, surpassing human potential in discovery. Current AI development scenarios may underestimate this potential, as iterative improvements in coding and research automation could accelerate progress toward such transformative outcomes.

results.title: Evolution has optimized fine motor coordination so well. Even if you look at robot hands, the ability to open a door is still just really hard for robots. Meanwhile, we're seeing this total automation of coding, and everything else that we've seen as clever.
